evidence: V committed to escalating: 'I'll talk to the board.' This indicates hiring requires board approval (likely headcount/budget implications).
Ray's advice—'If you only have one, don't expect to make any money'—reveals that acquisition value is created primarily by competition, not by the intrinsic value of what's being sold.
In M&A, you're not selling a company. You're selling the fear of losing it to a competitor. The product is FOMO, not technology.
Ray's experience shows acquirers can manipulate success metrics. He made $50-100K vs potential $1M due to earnout structure.
What looks like incentive alignment is often risk transfer. When the party defining success metrics is the same party measuring them, 'alignment' becomes a polite word for 'control'.
The meta-lesson: salary is the only honest signal. Optimize for salary and bonuses rather than earnout structures.

The gap between what engineers assume and what's actually needed for business goals is a leading indicator of communication health. V clarified that acquirer demos have lower requirements than Ilse assumed.
Kathy Pham, VP, Head of AI at Workday. Logan knows her directly.
Logan knows her directly. Long shot but reachable.

source_display: Multiple Competing Offers as Existential Goal
evidence: Ray's advice reframed the goal: not 'get acquired' but 'get 2-3 parties competing.' This changes how the team will allocate outreach energy.

source_display: Better at Job = Worse at Interview Paradox
evidence: The better you are at a job, the worse you are at interviewing because you've been fucking employed, because people have not wanted to let you go.

source_display: Better at Job = Worse at Interview Paradox
evidence: Interview skill and job skill are inversely correlated at the high end. We've built a system that selects for people who are good at *leaving* jobs, not *doing* them.
You're essentially reallocating a lot of low grade assets that are filtering and vetting your candidates to maybe a smaller number of higher grade employees.

Think of organizational attention as a portfolio of assets. Most companies allocate their lowest-value cognitive resources to their highest-stakes decisions. This is the equivalent of having interns manage your investment portfolio.

Candidates who can articulate their experience as coherent stories get dramatically better scores. The story *is* the product. A candidate with 6 rich stories beats a candidate with 20 years of undifferentiated experience.
We're not actually measuring competence — we're measuring narrative fluency. Coaching candidates on story development might have 10x the ROI of skill development.
Shivam is building an AI talent sourcing operation focused on India-based AI engineers. Target market: $700 billion (AI talent/staffing market).
V demonstrated Careerspan's capabilities. Partnership exploration continues - complementary capabilities (Shivam's network + Careerspan's intelligence layer).
Shivam: 'Employers have all the power. It's bullshit.' V: 'That's so shitty for candidates, man. Like, you can't even get feedback.'
We actually generate up to 20 to 30 pages of reasoning traces. For any given claim we can always look back to the specific part of what story that person said.
